Query: Does gepotidacin activate bacterial topoisomerase?

Options:
A: yes
B: no
Retrieved Documents:
Document [0] (Title: Cystitis -- Pearls and Other Issues) Gepotidacin is a new antibiotic in a novel chemical class called triazaacenaphthylene. [57] It selectively binds and inhibits bacterial topoisomerase IV and DNA gyrase, preventing or significantly slowing bacterial DNA replication. [58] [59] Unlike fluoroquinolones, gepotidacin contains no fluorine, appears not to cause any arthropathy, and utilizes a unique mechanism of action. [58] [59] It is effective against many target organisms like E. coli and Neisseria gonorrhea that are resistant to current antibiotics. [57] [60] If approved by the FDA, it would be the first new antibiotic for UTIs in over 20 years. The initial indication is likely for simple UTIs as its potential clinical application in complicated urinary tract infections, prostatitis, and urinary sepsis is still to be determined.

Document [3] (Title: Bacterial DNA Mutations -- Clinical Significance -- DNA Synthesis Inhibitor) Gram-negative bacterial resistance to fluoroquinolones includes the accumulation of substitution mutations in the coding regions for particular subunits of DNA topoisomerase II. Resistance can be enhanced further by efflux pump modification. [19] Ciprofloxacin targets only the parC subunit while other quinolones target one or more of these subunits. [20] For example, garenoxacin targets both DNA topoisomerases II and IV thus is less prone to resistance. Resistance to Garenoxacin requires both proteins to have resistance mutations. [21]

Document [8] (Title: Quinolones -- Mechanism of Action) Quinolones are bactericidal antibiotics that directly kill bacterial cells. They act on bacterial type II topoisomerases, DNA gyrase, and topoisomerase IV, inhibiting their function and converting them into toxic enzymes that produce permanent double-stranded breaks in the bacterial chromosome. DNA topoisomerases are essential for normal physiologic functions of the bacteria, such as DNA replication, transcription, recombination, and condensed DNA remodeling. They function by performing transient single- and double-stranded breaks, which help to facilitate their fundamental roles in removing torsional stress and knots in the bacterial chromosome that form during regular nucleic acid processes. Quinolone antibiotics stabilize the enzyme-DNA cleavage complexes by inhibiting DNA ligation. When the gyrase and topoisomerase IV create breaks in the bacterial chromosome to perform their physiological functions, it leads to fragmentation of the bacterial chromosome. When the DNA strand breaks overwhelm the cell’s ability to repair the DNA, it leads to cell death. [2] [3] [9] [11]

Document [11] (Title: Anthracyclines -- Mechanism of Action -- Enzyme Interaction) The most widely accepted explanation for the action of anthracyclines is their interaction with topoisomerase-II. The ternary complex thus formed prevents the re-ligation of the ds-DNA breaks. Subsequently, it promotes growth arrest and apoptotic cell death. [6]
Document [12] (Title: Ofloxacin -- Mechanism of Action) As with other second-generation fluoroquinolone drugs, ofloxacin is a broad-spectrum antimicrobial that exhibits a bactericidal effect. It works by binding to and inhibiting bacterial topoisomerase II (DNA gyrase), an enzyme that relaxes supercoiled DNA, and topoisomerase IV, an enzyme that separates linked daughter chromosomes following replication. These inhibitory effects interrupt DNA replication, transcription, and repair, thereby preventing cell division in bacterial cells. [6] In addition, fluoroquinolones are bactericidal and are concentrated intracellularly, resulting in rapid intracellular killing.

Document [25] (Title: Moxifloxacin -- Mechanism of Action) Moxifloxacin exerts its pharmacological effect by inhibiting bacterial DNA synthesis and replication. Moxifloxacin specifically targets 2 crucial enzymes involved in the bacterial DNA replication process—DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV. By binding to these enzymes, moxifloxacin prevents their ability to unwind and repair DNA strands, resulting in DNA damage and the inhibition of bacterial growth. This mechanism disrupts the bacteria's capacity to reproduce and survive, rendering moxifloxacin effective against a wide range of bacterial infections. [6]

Anagrelide’s antineoplastic effect is mediated by phosphodiesterase inhibition, illustrating enzyme‑targeted mechanisms within this class. The FOLFIRI chemotherapy regimen incorporates irinotecan, a topoisomerase I inhibitor, while methotrexate sodium acts as an antifolate that interferes with DNA synthesis. Talimogene laherparepvec represents an oncolytic viral therapy derived from human herpesvirus 1, highlighting virotherapy as a distinct antitumor strategy. Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 67) Actinomycin is a cytotoxic antibiotic that binds the DNA minor groove and is placed in the trabektedin class; it shares a broader depsipeptidy relationship and a use relationship with daktinomycin. Anthracyclines form a subclass of cytotoxic antibiotics and include aklarubicin, daunomycin, epirubicin (cited in the ET protocol) and idarubicin. Ixabepilone and mithramycin are additional cytotoxic antibiotics, with ixabepilone acting as a tubulin modulator. Plant‑derived antineoplastic agents are grouped under plant alkaloids and other natural‑product antineoplastic drugs. Within this group, colchicine derivatives such as demekolcin and podophyllotoxin derivatives are classified as antineoplastic drugs; the latter subclass comprises etoposide and teniposide, both derived from mutagenic compounds, with etoposide employed in the R‑ICE chemotherapy regimen and linked to the TCCSG B‑8801 protocol. Taxanes, another plant‑alkaloid subclass, include docetaxel, while Topoisomerase 1 inhibitors include topotecan. All these agents—actinomycin, anthracyclines, ixabepilone, mithramycin, colchicine derivatives, podophyllotoxin derivatives, taxanes, and TOP1 inhibitors—are organized into families that reflect their mechanisms of action (DNA binding, tubulin modulation, topoisomerase inhibition) and their incorporation into specific cancer‑treatment protocols.
Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 38) Actinomycin is a cytotoxic antibiotic that possesses a depsipeptide‑related structure and functions as a DNA minor‑groove binding agent. As a DNA minor‑groove binding agent, Actinomycin belongs to the trabektedin family, a broader group that also includes other plant alkaloids and natural products classified in ATC. The trabektedin family therefore encompasses both Actinomycin and various plant‑derived alkaloids that share minor‑groove binding activity. Anthracyclines and related substances constitute another major class of cytotoxic antibiotics. This class includes the individual agents aklarubicin, daunomycin, epirubicin and idarubicin, and it is linked to the compound mitoxantron as a member of the same therapeutic group. Epirubicin, in particular, serves as the basis for the ET protocol, illustrating its clinical application in chemotherapy regimens. Other cytotoxic antibiotics listed in ATC comprise ixabepilone and mithramycin. Ixabepilone is specifically identified as a tubulin modulator, highlighting its mechanism of disrupting microtubule dynamics. Both ixabepilone and mithramycin are categorized under the broader “cytotoxic antibiotics and related substances” hierarchy. Together, these relationships delineate a network of cytotoxic agents—spanning depsipeptide antibiotics, minor‑groove binders, anthracycline derivatives, and tubulin‑modulating compounds—each situated within larger pharmacologic families (trabektedin, anthracyclines, and general cytotoxic antibiotics) that inform their classification, mechanism of action, and therapeutic use.
